nodes.name: TIME Person of the Year
nodes.description: Notable TIME Person of the Year honorees since 1927

items.prompt: Who was the very first TIME Man of the Year in 1927, honored for completing the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight from New York to Paris?
items.explanation:
First-ever TIME Man of the Year, appearing on the January 2, 1928 cover
Flew the Spirit of St. Louis solo from New York to Paris on May 20–21, 1927 — 3,600 miles in 33.5 hours
Was just 25 years old; held the youngest Person of the Year record until Greta Thunberg (age 16) in 2019
The award was created because TIME had no obvious cover subject — an editor suggested picking a "Man of the Year" instead of a "Man of the Week"
His infant son was kidnapped and murdered in 1932 — called the "Crime of the Century"
Became controversial for his isolationist stance and acceptance of a medal from Nazi Germany in 1938
The award was originally called "Man of the Year" and renamed "Person of the Year" in 1999

items.prompt: Which Indian independence leader was TIME's Man of the Year in 1930, the year he led a 240-mile march to the sea to protest the British salt tax?
items.explanation:
Named Man of the Year in 1930 for the Salt March (March–April 1930); TIME's cover story was titled "Saint Gandhi"
Marched 240 miles from Sabarmati Ashram to the coastal village of Dandi to make salt in defiance of British law
Some 60,000 people were jailed during the subsequent salt satyagraha campaign
Pioneered nonviolent resistance (satyagraha), influencing Martin Luther King Jr. and Nelson Mandela
Assassinated January 30, 1948 by Hindu nationalist Nathuram Godse
Trained as a lawyer in London; spent 21 years in South Africa before returning to India
Named TIME's runner-up for Person of the Century in 1999 (behind Albert Einstein)

items.prompt: Which American president holds the record as the only person named TIME's Person of the Year three times (1932, 1934, 1941)?
items.explanation:
Only person ever named TIME Person of the Year three times: 1932 (president-elect), 1934 (New Deal), 1941 (WWII entry)
32nd President (1933–1945); only president elected four times before the 22nd Amendment imposed term limits
First selection in 1932 came after his landslide victory over incumbent Herbert Hoover
1934 selection recognized the New Deal programs aimed at Depression recovery
1941 selection followed Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor and U.S. entry into World War II
Died in office on April 12, 1945, just weeks before Germany's surrender
Served longer than any other president: 12 years and 39 days

items.prompt: Which American socialite was TIME's first-ever Woman of the Year in 1936, chosen because a king abdicated his throne to marry her?
items.explanation:
Named Woman of the Year in 1936 — the first female individual to receive the honor
King Edward VIII abdicated on December 11, 1936, declaring he could not reign "without the help and support of the woman I love"
The abdication crisis shook the British Empire; she was a twice-divorced American, unacceptable as queen consort
Became the Duchess of Windsor after their marriage on June 3, 1937
The couple lived in exile, mostly in France, for the rest of their lives
Edward's abdication made his brother George VI king — the father of Queen Elizabeth II
Born Bessie Wallis Warfield in Blue Ridge Summit, Pennsylvania in 1896

items.prompt: Which German dictator, who annexed Austria and dismembered Czechoslovakia through the Munich Agreement, was TIME's most controversial Man of the Year choice in 1938?
items.explanation:
Named Man of the Year in 1938 — widely considered the most controversial selection in the award's history
TIME stated he "became in 1938 the greatest threatening force that the democratic, freedom-loving world faces today"
The cover depicted him playing a cathedral organ with dead bodies hanging from a wheel overhead — clearly not an endorsement
Selected for annexing Austria (Anschluss, March 1938) and forcing the Munich Agreement (September 1938)
The Munich Conference with Chamberlain, Daladier, and Mussolini ceded the Sudetenland to Germany
TIME has repeatedly clarified the award recognizes influence, not approval: "for better or for worse"
World War II began less than a year later with Germany's invasion of Poland on September 1, 1939

items.prompt: Which Soviet dictator was named TIME's Man of the Year twice (1939, 1942) — first for the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, then for leading the defense against Nazi invasion?
items.explanation:
Named Man of the Year twice: 1939 (for the Nazi-Soviet pact) and 1942 (for the Soviet war effort)
1939 selection followed the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with Germany, which shocked the world and enabled the invasion of Poland
1942 selection recognized the Soviet Union's defense, particularly the Battle of Stalingrad (1942–1943)
General Secretary of the Communist Party from 1922 until his death on March 5, 1953
His regime killed millions through forced collectivization, purges, and the Gulag system
Attended the Yalta and Potsdam conferences that shaped the post-war world order
Nikita Khrushchev denounced his "cult of personality" in the Secret Speech of 1956

items.prompt: Which British wartime leader was named TIME's Man of the Year in 1940 and later received the special designation "Man of the Half-Century" in 1949?
items.explanation:
Named Man of the Year for 1940 and Man of the Half-Century for 1949 — the first special "Half-Century" designation
Became Prime Minister on May 10, 1940, the same day Germany invaded France and the Low Countries
His speeches ("We shall fight on the beaches," "Their finest hour") rallied British morale during the Blitz
Served two non-consecutive terms as PM: 1940–1945 and 1951–1955
Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1953 for his historical and biographical writing, not for politics
Lost the 1945 general election to Clement Attlee despite leading Britain to victory in Europe
Died January 24, 1965; his state funeral drew the largest assembly of world leaders to that date

items.prompt: Which 25-year-old royal, who ascended to the British throne in February 1952 after her father's death, was named TIME's Woman of the Year that same year?
items.explanation:
Named Woman of the Year for 1952, the year she became queen at age 25 after the death of George VI on February 6
Her coronation on June 2, 1953 at Westminster Abbey was the first to be televised, watched by an estimated 27 million UK viewers
Reigned for 70 years and 214 days (1952–2022) — the longest-reigning British monarch in history
Her accession was a direct consequence of the 1936 abdication: Edward VIII's departure put her father on the throne
Died September 8, 2022 at Balmoral Castle in Scotland at age 96
Served alongside 15 British prime ministers, from Churchill to Liz Truss
Her Silver (1977), Golden (2002), Diamond (2012), and Platinum (2022) Jubilees were major national celebrations

items.prompt: Which Baptist minister and civil rights leader, who delivered the "I Have a Dream" speech at the March on Washington, was named TIME's Man of the Year for 1963?
items.explanation:
Named Man of the Year for 1963, the year of the March on Washington and his "I Have a Dream" speech
Led the August 28, 1963 March on Washington, where an estimated 250,000 people gathered at the Lincoln Memorial
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1964 at age 35 — at the time, the youngest recipient
Led the Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–1956), the Birmingham campaign (1963), and the Selma marches (1965)
Assassinated April 4, 1968 at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee at age 39
His birthday (January 15) became a federal holiday in 1986 — the first honoring an African American
Inspired by Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolent resistance after visiting India in 1959

items.prompt: Which paramount leader of China, architect of sweeping economic reforms that blended communism with market capitalism, was named TIME's Person of the Year twice (1978, 1985)?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year twice: 1978 (for launching modernization) and 1985 (for deepening market reforms)
1978 selection recognized his "Four Modernizations" program for agriculture, industry, science/technology, and defense
1985 selection cited his "sweeping economic reforms that have challenged Marxist orthodoxies"
Effectively led China from 1978 to 1992 despite never holding the title of head of state or head of government
Ordered the Tiananmen Square crackdown on June 4, 1989, killing hundreds to thousands of pro-democracy protesters
Famous for the pragmatic saying: "It doesn't matter whether a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice"
Died February 19, 1997, just months before the handover of Hong Kong from Britain to China

items.prompt: In 1982, TIME broke tradition by naming a non-human as its "Machine of the Year" — what device was it, described as transforming offices, homes, and daily life?
items.explanation:
Named "Machine of the Year" for 1982 — the first non-human entity to receive the designation
TIME chose it over any individual, recognizing technology's transformative impact on society
The IBM PC had launched in August 1981, establishing the standard for personal computing
The Commodore 64 also launched in 1982, becoming the best-selling single computer model in history
Apple had introduced the Apple II in 1977 and would unveil the Macintosh in January 1984
The choice inspired a second non-human selection: "The Endangered Earth" was named Planet of the Year in 1988
TIME's managing editor said "no single individual more dominated the year" than this machine

items.prompt: Which Soviet leader, known for glasnost and perestroika, was named TIME's Man of the Year twice (1987, 1989) and received the special title "Man of the Decade"?
items.explanation:
Named Man of the Year for 1987 and 1989; his 1989 selection carried the special designation "Man of the Decade"
1987 selection called him "a symbol of hope for a new kind of Soviet Union"
1989 selection credited him as "the force behind the most momentous events of the '80s"
Introduced glasnost (openness) and perestroika (restructuring), which inadvertently hastened the Soviet Union's collapse
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1990 for his role in ending the Cold War
Oversaw the fall of the Berlin Wall (November 9, 1989) and German reunification (October 3, 1990)
The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991; he died on August 30, 2022 at age 91

items.prompt: In 2002, TIME named three women — Cynthia Cooper, Coleen Rowley, and Sherron Watkins — as its Persons of the Year for exposing fraud and institutional failures at Enron, WorldCom, and the FBI. What collective title did TIME give them?
items.explanation:
Named Persons of the Year for 2002 as "The Whistleblowers" — three women who exposed major institutional failures
Sherron Watkins warned Enron chairman Kenneth Lay about improper accounting before the company's collapse
Cynthia Cooper exposed WorldCom's $3.8 billion accounting fraud, the largest corporate fraud at that time
Coleen Rowley, an FBI staff attorney, revealed that the bureau ignored warnings about Zacarias Moussaoui before 9/11
TIME praised them "for believing — really believing — that the truth is one thing that must not be moved off the books"
All three testified before Congress; their actions helped spur the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002
First time three women shared the Person of the Year designation

items.prompt: In 2006, TIME placed a reflective Mylar mirror on its Person of the Year cover, honoring the millions of people creating content on YouTube, Wikipedia, and MySpace. What one-word answer was printed on the cover?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year for 2006 — the cover featured a reflective Mylar mirror so readers saw themselves
Honored the rise of user-generated content on platforms like YouTube (founded 2005), Wikipedia, MySpace, and Facebook
Editor Richard Stengel: the mirror "literally reflects the idea that you, not us, are transforming the information age"
The selection was widely mocked as a cop-out; critic Paul Kedrosky called it a "near-term market top for user-generated content"
YouTube had been acquired by Google for $1.65 billion in October 2006
Facebook had opened registration beyond college students in September 2006
Now seen as prescient: TIME later acknowledged the choice "seemed gimmicky at the time but proved visionary"

items.prompt: Which U.S. senator from Illinois, who became the first African American elected president, was named TIME's Person of the Year twice (2008, 2012)?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year twice: 2008 (first election) and 2012 (re-election)
44th President (2009–2017); first African American to hold the office
2008 selection followed his historic victory over John McCain with 365 electoral votes
2012 selection followed his re-election over Mitt Romney
Signed the Affordable Care Act (2010), ordered the raid that killed Osama bin Laden (2011), and normalized relations with Cuba (2014)
Won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2009, controversially, less than a year into his first term
One of several presidents chosen as both president-elect and sitting president, following FDR

items.prompt: Which Argentine-born Jesuit, elected as the first head of the Catholic Church from the Americas, was named TIME's Person of the Year in 2013?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year for 2013, praised "for pulling the papacy out of the palace and into the streets"
First Jesuit pope, first pope from the Americas, and first pope from the Southern Hemisphere
Elected on March 13, 2013, taking the name Francis after St. Francis of Assisi — the first pope to choose that name
Third pope named Person of the Year, after John XXIII (1962) and John Paul II (1994)
Born Jorge Mario Bergoglio in Buenos Aires, Argentina on December 17, 1936 to an Italian immigrant father and Argentine-born mother of Italian descent
Known for a more informal papal style: chose to live in the Vatican guesthouse rather than the Apostolic Palace
Died April 21, 2025 at age 88; served as pope for over 12 years

items.prompt: In 2014, TIME honored the doctors, nurses, and health workers risking their lives in West Africa to contain a deadly viral epidemic. What collective name did the cover use?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year for 2014 as "The Ebola Fighters" — honoring the health workers combating the West African epidemic
The 2014–2016 outbreak killed over 11,000 people across Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone
Five individual health workers appeared on the cover, representing thousands who risked their lives on the front lines
TIME cited them "for tireless acts of courage and mercy, for buying the world time to boost its defenses"
The epidemic spread to Nigeria, Mali, Senegal, Spain, the United Kingdom, and the United States, triggering global panic
Dr. Kent Brantly and Nancy Writebol became the first Ebola patients treated in the U.S., at Emory University Hospital
The WHO declared the outbreak over on June 9, 2016

items.prompt: Which real estate mogul and TV personality, who defeated Hillary Clinton in a surprise 2016 upset, was named TIME's Person of the Year that year and again in 2024 after a historic political comeback?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year twice: 2016 (first election) and 2024 (second election)
45th and 47th President; only president since Grover Cleveland to serve non-consecutive terms
2016 cover read "President of the Divided States of America"; he won the Electoral College 304–227 while losing the popular vote
2024 selection cited his "comeback of historic proportions" and "once-in-a-generation political realignment"
Beat shortlists that included Vladimir Putin (2016) and Kamala Harris (2024)
The only person impeached twice by the House of Representatives (2019 and 2021)
Host of The Apprentice (2004–2015); his catchphrase "You're fired" became a cultural phenomenon

items.prompt: Which 16-year-old Swedish climate activist, who started a solo school strike outside parliament in 2018, became the youngest-ever TIME Person of the Year in 2019?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year for 2019 at age 16 — the youngest individual ever to receive the honor
Broke the record previously held by Charles Lindbergh, who was 25 when named in 1927
Began her "Skolstrejk för klimatet" (School Strike for Climate) alone outside the Swedish parliament in August 2018
Her activism sparked the global Fridays for Future movement, with millions of students striking worldwide
Famously told world leaders at the UN Climate Action Summit in September 2019: "How dare you"
Diagnosed with Asperger syndrome, which she has called her "superpower"
First Person of the Year born in the 21st century (born January 3, 2003)

items.prompt: Which former comedian and actor, elected president of his country in 2019, was named TIME's 2022 Person of the Year alongside "the spirit of" his nation after it was invaded by Russia?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year for 2022 alongside "the spirit of Ukraine" after Russia's full-scale invasion on February 24
TIME cited him "for proving that courage can be as contagious as fear" and "for reminding the world of the fragility of democracy"
Refused to flee Kyiv, reportedly telling the U.S.: "I need ammunition, not a ride"
Posted a 40-second Instagram video on February 25, 2022 showing his cabinet intact and in place in Kyiv
Starred in the Ukrainian TV series Servant of the People (2015–2019), playing a teacher who becomes president
Elected president of Ukraine in April 2019 with 73% of the vote, defeating incumbent Petro Poroshenko
Delivered virtual addresses to dozens of world parliaments, the World Bank, and even the Grammy Awards

items.prompt: Which pop megastar, whose record-breaking Eras Tour became the first concert tour to gross over $1 billion, was named TIME's Person of the Year for 2023?
items.explanation:
Named Person of the Year for 2023 — the first entertainer to receive the designation solo in the award's 96-year history
The Eras Tour became the first concert tour to gross over $1 billion; average attendee reportedly spent nearly $1,300
Became a billionaire in 2023, driven by tour revenue, re-recorded albums, and streaming dominance
The Eras Tour concert film became the highest-grossing concert movie in history
Her Glendale, Arizona tour stop generated more local revenue than the 2023 Super Bowl held in the same stadium
Became Spotify's most-streamed artist globally in 2023, ending Bad Bunny's three-year reign
Re-recorded her early albums ("Taylor's Version") to reclaim ownership after a publicized dispute over her masters
